speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 16, 1996. The survivor who is going to be interviewed is Kurt Moses. His name at birth was Kurt Moses. The interviewer is Dr. Pat Sarah Padva. The Interview is occurring in Pompano Beach, Florida, USA. And the interview will be in English. This is tape one of an interview on January 16, 1996, with Mr. Kurt Moses. We are doing the interview in Pompano Beach, Florida. And would you like to tell us your name and spell it, and your name at birth, and your age, and your birth date, and your city and country of birth? And spell those, if you would.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. My name is Kurt, spelled K-U-R-T, Moses, M-O-S-E-S. I was born July 18, 1925, in Linnich, Germany. And Linnich is spelled L-I-N-N-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your age and birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age is 70. I was born July 18, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Moses, what was life like for you before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say where we lived in this little town, in Linnich, population approximately 2,000, was like any other little town where a child grew up. It was not much excitement and a very normal life. My family consisted of my parents and my older sister. And we lived, looking back, in very good circumstances. My father had a business, like a department store, which he had inherited from his parents, who died in Linnich. They're buried in Linnich in a cemetery. And comparatively, I would say we were in the upper middle class. We had a chauffeur. We had a Mercedes Benz then. We had a sleep-in maid. And we had employees working for us. And we used to go on regular vacations. And I would say, looking back, life was very easy, very good over there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had relatives that lived close by. And my dad was active in the community. He was a veteran of the First World War. He had been two years in active duty when the war started. So actually, he was six years in the military, of which he was very proud. And he had what they called the Iron Cross, which was quite an accomplishment then. And I would say we were very happy people there, had been there for so many generations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until, of course , the change came when Hitler became Reichskanzler, when he was elected.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was 1933. And I remember my dad had a friend who went to one of his campaign speeches, to Hitler's campaign speeches. And I remember when he came back he told my mother, he said, you know, my friend told me this man is very dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, when he stood there and listened to him screaming and yelling, and he said this lady that stood next to him said to him, look at this man. Doesn't he remind you of God? And life started to change rapidly after he became the head of the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Linnich itself, we had-- Jewish life was great. We had no problems. We had friends. My friends were non-Jewish children. My parents socialized with non-Jewish friends. The word Jew never was a question. The only difference was on Sundays, they went to their church. And on Saturdays, we went to our temple. We had a great community life there. We had a rabbi, who was also the teacher. We had our own school. And there were only about 15, 18 Jewish families in the whole town. Remarkable thing was, they all had a business. Looking back, I don't think anybody had a job. Everybody had a little business somehow, whether it was the tailor or the shoemaker. I would say, all in all, it was a good life. We had a kosher butcher store. And our rabbi's name was Max Gottlieb. He was a man out in the community, very well-respected. Like I said, until gradually, the change came. And sure enough, we noticed people who became members of the Nazi party. And they formed their groups. And I remember when they first started marching and singing songs, anti-Semitic songs. And I remember when they passed our house in groups and marched. And they used to sing these anti-Semitic songs. I remember one very well. It said, when the Jewish blood spatters from our knives, that's when life will get better. And I remember my parents standing behind the curtains and watching who the people were. And every time it happened, they said, I can't believe that this one is also a member now. And I can't believe that this person joined the party, and so forth. And they must have become very depressed to see people that they had grew up with an associated with all of a sudden making such a turn. And then of course, the boycotting started. They erected signs that they shouldn't patronize and buy at Jewish establishments, and so forth. And it started to affect our business. I remember they posted a Nazi photographer in front of our business. And his job was to take pictures, to keep a record of who the people were that still were buying in Jewish stores.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all in all, with the whole package, my dad felt that it wasn't too good. And he couldn't see any way that it would get better for the time being. And he decided to move across the border, which we lived very close to the Dutch border. Linnich is very close, just a few miles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he did have business connections with people in Holland. So it wasn't a big thing for us to just move the business over to the Dutch side. And this is what we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, in the Jewish community it was quite an upset. Because we were the first ones. Nobody ever heard of anybody leaving this town. And they kind of were upset. And I asked them, what are you afraid of? Are you chicken? Just because this man is there, you want to run away from it? After all, you have an obligation to be here and so forth. But he decided to move. And we did move to the Dutch side. What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still going to school in Germany before you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had this Jewish school there. We had our own school. And actually, I only-- you know, I was about seven or eight years old, so maybe around one or maybe close to two years that I went to school there. But I still remember the German language. This teacher we had there was very professional. And whatever he taught me, I, I still remember. And then, of course, in Holland, everything was Dutch. That's really where I grew up. And I spent most of my time in Holland, so to speak of.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city did you move to in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved first to a little town called [? Flein, ?] which is also across the border, and later to Sittard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sittard, S-I-T-T-A-R-D-- and Sittard is a border town. It's half a mile from the-- you can walk. We used to walk from Holland into Germany. So it's-- the people in that area-- there weren't any great limitations-- Used to cross the border as they wanted to. And Holland, life was beautiful before the war. We had a furniture factory in Holland and-- which was very successful. And we had a great life there. And, of course, everybody was hoping that the, the end of the Nazis wouldn't be too far away. And then things would go back to normal. Of course, as we know, that wasn't the case. But we lived very peacefully. And we really-- I would say if things would have stayed normal, I don't think we would have gone back to Germany. Because life in Holland was really good. And the people were great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a lot of friends in Holland. I still have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish and non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish, yeah. We did have a temple in Sittard too. And we had a rabbi. His name was [? Van Bleidenstein ?]. He was a very gifted man, a great violinist besides. And life continued where we left off, so to speak of. And it was just beautiful. And then, of course, on May 10, 1940, the German invasion happened. And, of course, since we were five minutes from the border, Sittard was the first town, so to speak of, that was occupied by the German occupation forces. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had tried to escape. There were rumors that there might be an invasion. And several times, we, we tried to leave . But then it was only a rumor. And people said, well, see, nothing really happened. It's just talk. And of course, we went back to normal life again. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people really didn't take it too serious. They really never expected that Germany would invade countries like Holland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when it did happen, it really came as a surprise. And then, of course, it was too late. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After we were established in Holland, my father convinced the rest of our family they should do the same thing we did. It would be better, for that period of time, to be in Holland. And my mother was one of nine children. And most of the family, he was able to convince. And they did come across the border. And they did start the life again in Holland. And one was a butcher. He had a butcher store. And one was-- I remember, sister was married to a man. He was in the cattle business. And he started his cattle business again there. And life was great. And my mother's parents also came over and lived with-- in our area. So life was really beautiful again, until the invasion. Then again, it started to change. You could see a change. The German propaganda started their work, which was very professional. And you had a Dutch Nazi party. And you did have Dutch Nazis, all of a sudden, that you didn't think would become Nazis. But they did. And looking back, I believe that this was more of an economic anti-Semitism, people that saw that they had an advantage [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] financially by being-- becoming Nazis. But they were there. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of course, the first year wasn't that bad, because they had to do a lot of fighting. They went to Belgium and France and-- but after they-- this was all, so to speak of, taken care of, then, of course, they concentrated on the every day's life. And they started what we call the Jew laws. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jew laws, I would say, started with we were not allowed to visit public places, like movies, concerts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] museums, and so forth. There was a curfew. We were not allowed to be on the street after 8:00 at night. And then, of  course, we had to wear the Jew star to identify ourselves all the time that we were Jews. And the reaction of the Dutch population was, was beautiful. I remember when I first wore that Jewish star, people that I never knew and never paid attention to all of a sudden gave me this friendly hello. And they used the word [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That means, you know, just be strong. It's not going to last, you know, that long, anyway. It's just a period we have to try to get over. And I remember the whole country went on a nationwide strike to demonstrate that they do not approve of the way the Nazis treat their Jewish citizens. But then of course, the Germans were capable of handling situations like that. And they arrested many good people and sent them to concentration camps. And many of them did not return.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly non-Jews, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember the Germans took over a Jewish bank in Amsterdam called Lippmann, Rosenthal. And all Jews in Holland had to send their-- transfer their bank accounts, savings accounts, business accounts, to this particular bank. All their holdings, all their stocks, or bonds, or whatever, birth certificates, everything had to be sent to Lippmann, Rosenthal, which then, of course, was taken over by the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Things became known that eventually, Jews would be deported. But it was presented in such a way that, OK, here we are, your masters, so to speak of. And we need labor in Germany. Because they were fighting on the east front, and on the west front, and in Africa. And you will be forced to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] work for us until this war, of course, over, which we will win. Then will things probably go back to normal, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whoever thought of a final solution as it was planned. And we used to get instructions from what they called the [? Joodsche  Raad, ?] the Jewish committee. And they'd send us a list and that prepared us, so to speak of, in case you would be called for labor, [GERMAN SPEECH], labor force in Germany, how to prepare yourself. And it gave us a list of what to wear, what to take along. And I remember, we had special backpacks mad and special warm underwear. And we even had a little first aid kit and vitamins. So we were prepared, just in  case we were called. So we go to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: The question always was, so what? So you're still young. So we go to work. This is not going to last forever. He's not going to kill all the Jews. I mean, let's face it, this is-- you know. And in that order, we kind of prepared ourselves for the deportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, Jewish people gave their good things like your silverware or your valuables to their good non-Jewish friends, just to keep them until we come back. You know, it's not going to be that long anyway. And so did we. My-- I remember at night, we went to my father's foreman. He was the foreman of the factory, which for years, they were good friends. And one night, we packed the boxes. And we brought the silver over to their house to safe keep for us. This other good friend, his name was [? Yessen ?]. I remember, he-- we had a few gold pieces, which he kept for us. And I think-- I remember, my dad had some money, which he didn't send to Lippmann, Rosenthal. And I remember, they bought a little house. And they put it in his friend's name. Because it wasn't-- so we had some money, just in case, you know, when we come back, that we have something again to get started with. And so we really didn't think serious anything about going there. A lot of people went into hiding. And we had an opportunity also, the four of us, by good friends who wanted to hide us. The problem was that nobody was able to take four people in one household. So we had to split up. And the way it was presented was that my dad and I were supposed to go with one family, my mother and my sister with another family. These people were good people. Don't forget, food was rationed already. And we, of course, wouldn't get any coupons. So they had to share what they got with us. And this way, 8,000 people were saved that way in Holland during that period. However, my parents decided since we were not allowed to know where the other two were, and that was done-- the underground, you know, it was a dangerous assignment-- just in case one of us would get caught. And they would force us to tell them where the others are.

speaker: interviewee
text: So therefore, we were not supposed to know where, let's say, my father and I weren't supposed to know where my mother and sister were. Later of course, my parents decided against. And I felt they don't want to break up the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if we have to go, so we'll go like the rest of them. And you know, after all, the same thing-- we are still young. And we'll survive it. It's no problem. And sure enough, one day, we were-- they came to the house, like 1:00, midnight. And they marched us to the railroad station. And there was a special train. And we were deported. We were sent to a camp up north called Westerbork. Westerbork was a-- I would say, a proper word would be a transient camp. All individual, all prisoners that were shipped to Germany or to Eastern Europe had to go through Westerbork. That's where they were registered, and so forth. And from Westerbork, the trains left every Tuesday to the east. And the unique thing about Westerbork was they had a huge hospital. And here again, if you arrived in Westerbork and you were sick, you were not assigned on a transport. First, you were sent to the hospital so make sure that you are well, to present the image that you are not good to us if you cannot produce. So first, let's cure you. And then we'll send you to the gas chambers and to be gassed. Of course, we all didn't know that at that time. But that was Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have difficulty with the time element. I remember, we arrived there on November 11, 1942. That's when we were deported. And when we arrived, I remember the commander of the camp. His name was Obersturmbannfuhrer Gemmeker. He was a German, like you see the cartoonists make him. And I was 17 years old at that time. And I was in good physical condition. And I was assigned as-- in the garbage collection group, which meant I got a little wheelbarrow and a barrel. And I had to go to the barracks and empty out the small barrels into the big barrel. And then I had to wheel them to the pile outside the camp where the garbage was. And it didn't bother me. I was able to handle that very easily. As a matter of fact, when I emptied out the garbage from the guard houses, I always found some extra food in there, which was still good. And you know, the food was always a problem. But lots of time, I found some stuff there that was still edible. And it didn't bother me. I was quite happy with this assignment. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your family in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Our family was still together there. And we all were assigned different jobs in this camp. And then they asked for people who knew a trade. And of course, I had become a cabinet maker after we weren't allowed to go to school anymore. I worked in our factory. And so I told them that I worked in a carpenter shop then. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] From Westerbork, we were sent to Theresienstadt in Czechoslovakia. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I don't know the exact time. I cannot give you the exact time.
